FT_IC1.evidence: Uses Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study data, 2016-2021

FT_IC2.evidence: Generic 'screen use' composite defined as sum of television, videos, video games, social media, video chat, and texting; no smartphone or social-media-specific variable extracted separately

FT_IC3.evidence: Descriptive growth mixture modeling only; groups characterized by trajectory patterns but no inferential statistical test linking screen use to non-SM outcome

FT_IC4.evidence: No β/OR/HR/r/CI reported for smartphone/SM association to external outcome

FT_IC5.evidence: Descriptive study characterizing trajectories and group demographics; no empirical analysis linking exposure to outcome

FT_IC6.evidence: Published journal article in Preventive Medicine Reports

notes: Descriptive vs. inferential: No hypothesis test; only means, SDs, and trajectory visualization.

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study baseline and follow-up data (11,876 children); validated in HCP-D independent sample

FT_IC2.evidence: Screen Media Activity (SMA) measured via 14-item ABCD Youth Screen Time Questionnaire capturing time on 6 types: watching TV/movies, playing video games, video chat, texting, and social networking; SMA patterns identified via K-means clustering

FT_IC3.evidence: Linear mixed effects (LME) modeling with FDR correction; SMA patterns linked to neurocognitive performance, behavioral problems, psychotic-like experiences, impulsivity, reward sensitivity, and resting-state brain functional connectivity

FT_IC4.evidence: Standardized β coefficients reported with 95% CI and FDR-corrected p-values: β = −.12 (95% CI [−0.08, −0.16], p < .001) for neurocognition; β = .13 (95% CI [0.09, 0.18], p < .001) for behavioral problems; β = .31 (95% CI [0.27, 0.36], p < .001) for psychotic-like experiences

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis: K-means clustering to identify SMA patterns, LME modeling to examine associations across ABCD baseline, 1-year, and 2-year follow-up; validated in HCP-D independent sample

FT_IC6.evidence: Published journal article in Journal of the American Academy of Child & Adolescent Psychiatry, 2023

FT_EC7_reason: SMA is primary independent variable, not dependent variable; includes individual psychological predictors (impulsivity, sensitivity to punishment/reward); analyses are both cross-sectional and longitudinal with non-SM outcomes
notes: Latent SMA patterns (K-means clusters) with separately extractable pathways to each outcome. ML/LME with reported coefficients qualify under E3 and E4.

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study data from 21 U.S. sites; consortium with over 10,000 youth enrolled at baseline (ages 9-10)

FT_IC2.evidence: Screen time mentioned as one predictor among many in machine learning models (included in feature weights); no separately reported coefficient or analysis isolating smartphone/SM association; focus is neurocognition/demographics predicting ADHD, not smartphone/SM as primary exposure

FT_IC3.evidence: Screen time included only as predictor in multi-domain ML model; no inferential test or coefficient reported specifically linking screen time to non-SM outcome independent of other predictors

FT_IC4.evidence: No β/OR/HR/r/CI reported for smartphone/SM association

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis: machine learning (PCR and LASSO) models predicting ADHD symptoms from neurocognitive, demographic, and child/family characteristics with cross-validation across ABCD sites

FT_IC6.evidence: Published journal article in Translational Psychiatry, 2023

FT_EC7_reason: Fails FT_IC2 (screen time not separately analyzed with reported coefficient)
notes: E1: Screen time as one predictor of many with no separately reported coefficient—fails FT_IC3. This is a feature importance paper where screen time is included in ML models but not separately analyzed as a smartphone/SM exposure of interest.
